host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-007.technique: Plug-and-jämför-rutinen: när alternativen är konkreta funktioner och frågan jämför två funktionsvärden, sätt in båda x-värdena och räkna direkt. Triggern är 'för vilken funktion gäller …' med konkreta uttryck.
host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-007.pitfall: Botemedlet mot 'jag känner igen formen, jag vet svaret': räkna mekaniskt. (x − 1)² ser 'växande' ut men minskar precis kring x = 1. Konstant ser 'fast' ut men kan kännas som 'växer inte negativt'.

pregrade_tactic.move: Räkna f(0) och f(1) för varje alternativ — den med f(1) > f(0) vinner; ingen mental genväg behövs.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-008.solution_path: Tre parenteser med vardera 2 termer ger 2^3=8 termer i utvecklingen. Alla 8 kombinationer är: ace, acf, ade, adf, bce, bcf, bde, bdf — svar D.

steps.title: Förstå problemet
steps.text: Du ska utveckla produkten (a+b)(c+d)(e+f) — alltså multiplicera ihop alla tre parenteser och samla upp varje enskild produkt-term som uppstår.

steps.title: Vad är distributiva lagen?
steps.text: Distributiva lagen säger att x(y+z)=xy+xz. Du multiplicerar in x på varje term inne i parentesen. Detta är grundregeln som driver hela utvecklingen.

steps.title: Generalisera distributiva lagen
steps.text: När du multiplicerar två summor parar du varje term i den ena med varje term i den andra: (a+b)(c+d)=ac+ad+bc+bd — fyra produkter eftersom 2\cdot 2=4.

steps.title: Räkna antalet termer i förväg
steps.text: Tre parenteser med 2 termer var ger 2\cdot 2\cdot 2=2^3=8 termer i den färdiga utvecklingen. Detta är ett snabbt sanity-check innan du börjar.

steps.title: Utveckla första två parenteserna
steps.text: Multiplicera först (a+b)(c+d): a\cdot c+a\cdot d+b\cdot c+b\cdot d=ac+ad+bc+bd. Detta är 4 termer, exakt som steg 3 förutsäger.

steps.title: Multiplicera med (e+f)
steps.text: Nu har du (ac+ad+bc+bd)(e+f). Varje av de 4 termerna ska multipliceras med både e och f, vilket ger 4\cdot 2=8 termer.

steps.title: Utveckla systematiskt — e-delen
steps.text: Multiplicera varje term med e: ac\cdot e=ace, ad\cdot e=ade, bc\cdot e=bce, bd\cdot e=bde.

steps.title: Utveckla systematiskt — f-delen
steps.text: Multiplicera varje term med f: ac\cdot f=acf, ad\cdot f=adf, bc\cdot f=bcf, bd\cdot f=bdf.

steps.title: Samla alla 8 termer
steps.text: Hela utvecklingen: ace+acf+ade+adf+bce+bcf+bde+bdf. Räkna: 8 termer, var och en är produkten av exakt en term från varje parentes.

steps.title: Tolka strukturen
steps.text: Varje term är ett trippelval: 'välj a eller b, välj c eller d, välj e eller f'. Det finns 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 sådana val — exakt en term per kombination.

steps.title: Verifiera mot alternativen
steps.text: Alternativ D listar exakt dessa 8 termer i alfabetisk ordning. Alla andra alternativ har fel antal termer eller saknar de blandade indexen.

steps.title: Slutsats
steps.text: Svaret är D. Insikten i en mening: utvecklingen av n parenteser med var m termer ger m^n termer, en för varje kombination av val från parenteserna.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är lätt att utveckla bara de första två parenteserna och sedan addera 'e + f' separat istället för att multiplicera in dem.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 6 visar att (e+f) MULTIPLICERAS med varje av de 4 termerna; e och f står aldrig fritt utan att vara faktorer i en trippel-produkt.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar är ofta att stanna vid 8 termer som ser ut att räkna 'rätt' — men där varje term bara har två bokstäver istället för tre.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 4 fastställer 2³ = 8 termer, MEN varje term ska ha exakt 3 bokstäver (en från varje parentes). B:s termer har bara 2.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid ace + bdf och tänker att bara 'ytterhörnen' räknas, som om utvecklingen vore en diagonal.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 9 listar alla 8 trippelkombinationer. ace och bdf är bara 2 av de 8 — de övriga 6 (acf, ade, adf, bce, bcf, bde) saknas.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-008.technique: Distributiv-utvidgning-rutinen: räkna ut antalet termer i förväg (m^n), expandera systematiskt två parenteser i taget, lista alla val-kombinationer. Triggern är 'utveckla en produkt av flera parenteser'.
host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-008.pitfall: Botemedlet mot 'glömde en parentes': skriv ner förväntat antal termer först (här 8). Om ditt facit har färre, har du missat en faktor.

pregrade_tactic.move: Räkna antalet termer som m^n innan du börjar, expandera systematiskt två parenteser i taget — det matchande alternativet har rätt antal termer med rätt antal bokstäver var.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-009.solution_path: Cirkelarea-formeln: A=\pi r^2. Ur 27=\pi r^2 följer r^2=27/\pi\approx 8{,}6, alltså r\approx 2{,}93. Diameter = 2r\approx 5{,}9, närmast 6 cm. Svar C.

steps.title: Förstå problemet
steps.text: En cirkel har area 27\;\text{cm}^2. Du ska bestämma diametern (sträckan tvärs igenom cirkeln genom centrum) som ett närmevärde i cm.

steps.title: Cirkelns areaformel
steps.text: Arean av en cirkel med radien r är A=\pi r^2. Här är \pi\approx 3{,}14 en konstant; resten av ekvationen handlar om radien.

steps.title: Sambandet diameter–radie
steps.text: Diametern är dubbla radien: d=2r. Det betyder att om du hittar r så är d=2r bara ett kort steg bort.

steps.title: Sätt in arean
steps.text: Insättning: 27=\pi r^2. Du har en ekvation med en obekant (r) — målet är att lösa ut r.

steps.title: Lös ut r²
steps.text: Dela båda sidor med \pi: r^2=\frac{27}{\pi}. Numeriskt: 27/3{,}14\approx 8{,}6.

steps.title: Dra kvadratroten ur
steps.text: För att få r från r^2 drar du kvadratroten: r=\sqrt{27/\pi}\approx\sqrt{8{,}6}.

steps.title: Uppskatta √8,6
steps.text: Du vet att 2{,}9^2=8{,}41 och 3{,}0^2=9{,}00. Eftersom 8,6 ligger mellan dessa, ligger \sqrt{8{,}6} mellan 2,9 och 3,0 — säg ≈ 2,93.

steps.title: Räkna diametern
steps.text: Diametern är dubbla radien: d=2r\approx 2\cdot 2{,}93=5{,}86 cm.

steps.title: Avrunda mot alternativen
steps.text: Alternativen är 1,5, 4,5, 6 och 9 cm. Värdet 5,86 cm ligger närmast 6 cm. Skillnaden till 4,5 är ca 1,4 cm; till 6 cm är 0,14 cm — mycket mindre.

steps.title: Verifiera med diameter 6
steps.text: Om d=6 så är r=3 och A=\pi\cdot 3^2=9\pi\approx 28{,}3 cm². Det stämmer väl med givna 27 (lite mer, vilket bekräftar att riktig diameter är något under 6).

steps.title: Tvärkontrollera alternativ D
steps.text: Om d=9 skulle r=4{,}5 och A=\pi\cdot 4{,}5^2\approx 63{,}6 cm² — drygt dubbelt så stor som 27. D är fel.

steps.title: Slutsats
steps.text: Svaret är C (6 cm). Insikten i en mening: när du har en cirkelarea och söker diametern, isolera r^2, dra roten, och dubbla — håll \pi med hela vägen.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är lätt att tappa π helt och räkna √27 ≈ 5,2 och sedan på något sätt få 1,5 från räknemissar.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 5 visar att π MÅSTE vara med: r² = 27/π, inte r² = 27. Utan π hamnar du i fel storleksordning.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar är ofta att räkna RADIEN (≈ 3 cm) men sedan av misstag glida till 4,5 cm, eller att räkna med d² = 27/π och få ≈ 5,9 men avrunda fel.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 8 räknar diametern korrekt: d = 2r ≈ 5,86 ≈ 6 cm. Värdet 4,5 är inte radien (som är ≈ 3), inte heller diametern.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid 9 efter att ha kvadrerat felaktigt, t.ex. d² = 27 + π eller liknande räknefel.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 11 verifierar: d = 9 ger A ≈ 63,6 cm², mer än dubbelt så stor som angivna 27 cm². Bara d ≈ 6 cm ger A ≈ 27.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-009.technique: Cirkel-bakvägen-rutinen: skriv A = πr², isolera r² = A/π, dra roten ur, dubbla för diametern. Triggern är 'känd cirkelarea + sök radie/diameter'.
host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-009.pitfall: Botemedlet mot 'glömde π': räkna alltid r² = A/π, aldrig r² = A. Faktorn π skiljer rätt storleksordning från fel.

pregrade_tactic.move: Isolera r² = A/π, dra roten, dubbla — håll π med i kalkylen hela vägen tills sista avrundningen.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-010.solution_path: Räkna HL: \frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{3-2}{6}=\frac{1}{6}. Sätt in: \frac{1}{3}-x=\frac{1}{6}, alltså x=\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{2-1}{6}=\frac{1}{6}. Svar C.

steps.title: Förstå problemet
steps.text: Ekvationen \frac{1}{3}-x=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3} har en obekant x. Du ska hitta det tal som gör vänster- och högerledet lika.

steps.title: Plan: förenkla högerledet först
steps.text: Höger sida är en differens mellan två bråk utan obekant. Räkna ut den först så får du ekvationen 'något känt − x = något känt' som är trivial att lösa.

steps.title: Bråk-subtraktion: gemensam nämnare
steps.text: För att subtrahera bråk behövs samma nämnare. Hitta minsta gemensamma nämnare (MGN) av 2 och 3. Eftersom 2\cdot 3=6 och \gcd(2,3)=1 är MGN = 6.

steps.title: Räkna om bråken
steps.text: Skriv \frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{6} (multiplicera täljare och nämnare med 3) och \frac{1}{3}=\frac{2}{6} (multiplicera med 2). Värdet bevaras eftersom du multiplicerar med 1.

steps.title: Subtrahera höger sida
steps.text: \frac{3}{6}-\frac{2}{6}=\frac{3-2}{6}=\frac{1}{6}. Då blir ekvationen \frac{1}{3}-x=\frac{1}{6}.

steps.title: Lös ut x
steps.text: För ekvationen a-x=b gäller x=a-b. Här är a=\frac{1}{3} och b=\frac{1}{6}, alltså x=\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{6}.

steps.title: Räkna 1/3 − 1/6
steps.text: Gör om \frac{1}{3}=\frac{2}{6}. Då blir \frac{2}{6}-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{6}.

steps.title: Varför blir x = 1/6 och inte −1/6?
steps.text: Eftersom \frac{1}{3}>\frac{1}{6} är differensen \frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{6} positiv. Felteckenfällan ('jag drar ju x åt höger') byter ofta tecken där det inte ska bytas.

steps.title: Verifiera
steps.text: Sätt in x=\frac{1}{6} i originalet: \frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{2-1}{6}=\frac{1}{6}. Höger sida: \frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{6}. ✓ Båda sidor är 1/6.

steps.title: Mönsterigenkänning
steps.text: Lägg märke till symmetrin: båda sidor blir \frac{1}{6}, och x=\frac{1}{6}. Det är ingen tillfällighet — när två sidor är konstruerade lika är x lika med differensen.

steps.title: Slutsats
steps.text: Svaret är C (1/6). Insikten i en mening: vid bråk-ekvationer, förenkla först den sida som inte har x; det reducerar problemet till ett enkelt 'a − x = b'-steg.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är lätt att tappa tecken vid omflyttning och tro att x flyttar 'med tecknet' så att x = −1/2.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 6 fastställer x = a − b, INTE x = −(a − b). Sätt in: 1/3 − (−1/2) = 5/6, inte 1/6 — alltså inte ekvationen.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar är ofta −1/6 om man råkar räkna högerledet baklänges som 1/3 − 1/2 = −1/6.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 5 räknar 1/2 − 1/3 = +1/6, inte −1/6. Ordningen i frågan är 1/2 minus 1/3.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid 1/2 om man av misstag flyttar 1/3 fel och ser ekvationen som x = 1/2.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 9 verifierar: x = 1/2 skulle ge VL = 1/3 − 1/2 = −1/6, men HL = 1/6. Sidor stämmer inte.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-010.technique: Förenkla-först-rutinen: räkna alltid ut den sida som inte har x först; ekvationen reduceras då till 'a − x = b' eller 'a + x = b'. Triggern är 'bråkekvation där x bara finns på en sida'.
host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-010.pitfall: Botemedlet mot teckenfällan: x = a − b, inte x = b − a. Skriv ekvationen om till x = ... innan du sätter in siffror.

pregrade_tactic.move: Räkna ut den sida som saknar x först, skriv om till x = a − b, sätt in siffror — verifiera genom att substituera tillbaka.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-011.solution_path: Längd per gram: \frac{130}{50} m/g. För 430 g garn: \frac{130}{50}\cdot 430 meter. Svar C.

steps.title: Förstå problemet
steps.text: Ett nystan med 130 m garn väger 50 g. Emma använder 430 g — du ska välja det UTTRYCK som beräknar antalet meter, inte räkna ut svaret.

steps.title: Vad är ett proportionellt samband?
steps.text: När två storheter ändras tillsammans i samma takt kallas det proportionellt. Här: dubbla vikten ger dubbel längd; halva vikten ger halv längd. Förhållandet meter-per-gram är konstant.

steps.title: Beräkna förhållandet
steps.text: Meter per gram (m/g): \frac{130\text{ m}}{50\text{ g}}=\frac{130}{50} m/g. Det är 'hur många meter du får per gram'.

steps.title: Tänk i enheter (enhetsanalys)
steps.text: Multiplicera m/g med g för att få m: [\text{m/g}]\cdot[\text{g}]=[\text{m}]. Enheterna 'g' tar ut varandra — kvar blir meter, vilket är vad vi söker.

steps.title: Multiplicera med 430 g
steps.text: För 430 g garn: \frac{130}{50}\cdot 430 meter. Detta är uttrycket frågan vill ha.

steps.title: Räkna ut värdet (kontroll)
steps.text: Bara för att veta: \frac{130}{50}=2{,}6 m/g, gånger 430 g = 2{,}6\cdot 430=1118 m. Rimligt: 430 g är ungefär 8,6 nystan à 130 m ≈ 1118 m.

steps.title: Varför inte 50/130?
steps.text: Förhållandet \frac{50}{130} har enheten g/m (gram per meter, ca 0,38 g/m). Multiplicerar du detta med 430 g får du [\text{g/m}]\cdot[\text{g}]=[\text{g}^2/\text{m}] — fel enhet.

steps.title: Eliminera alternativ A
steps.text: A = 50/130 · 430. Det är gram per meter gånger gram = fel enhet. Avvisas.

steps.title: Eliminera alternativ B
steps.text: B = 50/430 · 130. Här delas 50 (gram) med 430 (gram) → ett tal utan enhet — multiplicerat med 130 m blir meter, men det blir fel STORLEK eftersom 430/130 borde stå i täljaren.

steps.title: Eliminera alternativ D
steps.text: D = \frac{430}{130}\cdot 50. Det är (gram/meter ändå?) — nej, 430/130 ger ett tal, gånger 50 (g) blir 'gram'. Slutprodukt: gram, inte meter. Fel enhet.

steps.title: Bekräfta alternativ C
steps.text: C = \frac{130}{50}\cdot 430: (m/g)·g = m. Korrekt enhet, korrekt storlek (drygt 1100 m). ✓

steps.title: Slutsats
steps.text: Svaret är C. Insikten i en mening: vid proportionsuppgifter, sätt upp enhetskvoten så att 'fel-enhet' tar ut sig själv och rätt enhet återstår.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är lätt att räkna 50/130 (gram per meter) eftersom det ger ett 'litet trevligt' tal och sedan multiplicera med 430.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 8 visar att (g/m)·g = g²/m, inte meter. Du måste vända kvoten.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar är ofta 50/430 · 130 om man blandar ihop var 430 ska stå (täljare eller nämnare).
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 9 visar att 430 (den NYA vikten) ska multipliceras, inte stå i nämnare. Annars saknas hela storleksskalningen.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid 430/130 · 50 — man får ju ett tal med rätt 'känsla', och 50 multiplicerat ger något bekant.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 10 visar att slutprodukten har enheten gram (inte meter). Du har räknat ut 'hur många gram skulle 430/130 nystan väga' — fel storhet.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-011.technique: Enhetsanalys-rutinen: skriv upp varje storhets enhet (m, g, m/g), sätt upp kvoten så att den okända enheten kvarstår efter förkortning. Triggern är 'beräkna uttryck' i ett proportionellt samband.
host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-011.pitfall: Botemedlet mot 'kvot eller produkt?': skriv enheterna under siffrorna. Om slutprodukten har fel enhet är uttrycket fel oavsett hur 'rimligt' det ser ut.

pregrade_tactic.move: Skriv varje storhets enhet vid sidan, sätt upp kvoten så att de oönskade enheterna förkortas bort — det alternativ som ger m efter förkortning är rätt.

steps.title: Förstå problemet
steps.text: Uttrycket 4\cdot 2^x ska skrivas om till en form med EN potens. Du letar efter alternativet som är identiskt med uttrycket för alla x.

steps.title: Vad är en potens?
steps.text: Uttrycket a^n betyder 'a multiplicerat med sig själv n gånger': a^3=a\cdot a\cdot a. Här är a basen och n exponenten.

steps.title: Multiplikationsregeln för potenser
steps.text: Om baserna är samma adderas exponenterna: a^m\cdot a^n=a^{m+n}. Detta är nyckelregeln. Den fungerar bara om baserna är samma!

steps.title: Skriv om 4 som potens av 2
steps.text: Lägg märke till att 4=2\cdot 2=2^2. Detta är det avgörande steget: vi tvingar fram samma bas (2) i båda faktorerna.

steps.title: Skriv om uttrycket
steps.text: Med 4=2^2 blir 4\cdot 2^x=2^2\cdot 2^x. Nu har båda faktorerna samma bas (2) — multiplikationsregeln går att tillämpa.

steps.title: Tillämpa multiplikationsregeln
steps.text: Addera exponenterna: 2^2\cdot 2^x=2^{2+x}=2^{x+2}. Det är slututtrycket.

steps.title: Varför inte 2^(2x)?
steps.text: Regeln a^m\cdot a^n=a^{m+n} ADDERAR exponenter, multiplicerar dem inte. Att multiplicera exponenter motsvarar potens-av-potens: (a^m)^n=a^{mn} — en annan regel.

steps.title: Verifiera numeriskt vid x = 1
steps.text: Original: 4\cdot 2^1=4\cdot 2=8. Alternativ A: 2^{1+2}=2^3=8. ✓ Samma värde.

steps.title: Verifiera vid x = 3
steps.text: Original: 4\cdot 2^3=4\cdot 8=32. Alternativ A: 2^{3+2}=2^5=32. ✓

steps.title: Tvärkontrollera alternativ D (8^x)
steps.text: Vid x=1: 8^1=8 ✓ — slumpmässigt rätt. Vid x=2: 8^2=64 men original = 4\cdot 4=16. ✗ D fungerar inte generellt.

steps.title: Tvärkontrollera alternativ B (2^(2x))
steps.text: Vid x=1: 2^2=4; original = 8. ✗ B är fel.

steps.title: Slutsats
steps.text: Svaret är A. Insikten i en mening: när du multiplicerar potenser med olika 'utseende' men relaterade baser — skriv om allt till samma bas och addera exponenter.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är lätt att tro att man får 2^(2x) genom att 'multiplicera in 4' i exponenten, som om 4 vore en faktor på x.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 3 säger att man ADDERAR exponenter vid multiplikation, inte multiplicerar. Steg 7 förklarar varför.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar är ofta 4^(x-1) om man försöker 'flytta 4:an in i en exponent på fel sätt'.
distractors.why_wrong: 4^(x-1) = 4^x · 4^(-1) = 4^x/4 — det är inte samma som 4·2^x. Steg 8–9 verifierar att A (inte C) reproducerar uttrycket.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid 8^x av att tänka 4·2 = 8 och tro att 4·2^x = (4·2)^x.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 10 visar att 8^x bara råkar stämma vid x = 1; vid x = 2 ger 8^2 = 64 men originalet 16. Faktoriseringsregeln (ab)^x = a^x · b^x kräver att BÅDA är i exponent — här är bara 2 det.

host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-012.technique: Samma-bas-rutinen: när du multiplicerar potenser med olika baser, skriv om koefficienter och faktorer som potenser av samma bas, addera sedan exponenterna. Triggern är 'förenkla c · a^x' där c är en potens av a.
host-2022-kvant2-XYZ-012.pitfall: Botemedlet mot 'addera vs multiplicera exponenter': skriv ut regeln a^m · a^n = a^(m+n) i utkanten av räkningen innan du börjar. Det förhindrar både a^(mn)- och (ab)^x-felen.

pregrade_tactic.move: Skriv om varje koefficient som en potens av samma bas, addera exponenterna — verifiera med ett konkret x-värde.
On items about what was believed or known before event X, hunt for the contradicting 'although' sentence — it almost always carries the answer.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to assume early science 'couldn't see germs' — that's the classic textbook story of why disease was a mystery for so long.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly contradicts this: Leeuwenhoek observed tiny organisms in 1677, and scientists 'noted specific germs in the blood' before 1800. Step 2's locate step pulls those facts out plainly.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'they believed the microorganisms were a product of the illness' and conclude germs were thought to be unrelated to disease entirely.
distractors.why_wrong: 'A product of the illness' is still a RELATION — germs were thought to be CAUSED BY disease, not unrelated to it. B's 'not at all related' overshoots; step 3's paraphrase keeps the link intact (just reversed in direction).

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that 'germs in water' (Leeuwenhoek) and 'germs in blood' are two separate findings, so maybe nobody connected them.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage never makes that claim. It mentions both observations but never says scientists failed to link the water germs to the blood germs. Step 5's option-by-option pass shows C imports a claim the text never makes.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: Options like B ('not thought to be at all related to disease') sound like the right ballpark but overshoot. The text doesn't say germs were unrelated to disease in pre-1800 thinking — it says the relationship was misunderstood. Watch absolute words ('at all', 'never', 'always'); they often turn a near-match into a wrong answer.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'Germs' paragraph says scientists noted specific germs in the blood of sick people but 'believed the microorganisms were a product of the illness rather than the cause.' That is exactly what D states — the organisms were seen, just not blamed. Answer D.

steps.text: The prompt asks what is said about disease knowledge 'up until 1800'. So you need the option that names what scientists DID or DID NOT understand about disease before the 19th-century microscope improvements. Hold the timeframe firmly: pre-1800.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Three sentences sit before the 19th-century pivot. Hippocrates blamed 'bad air'. Then 'In a 1677 research paper, Dutch scientist Antoni van Leeuwenhoek observed tiny organisms, later identified as germs, in a droplet of water.' Then the key line: 'Although scientists noted specific germs in the blood of people with certain diseases, they believed the microorganisms were a product of the illness rather than the cause.'
steps.title: Locate the pre-1800 sentences

steps.text: Before 1800, germs were already being SEEN — Leeuwenhoek had spotted tiny organisms in water back in 1677, and scientists had even noticed specific germs in the blood of sick people. What they got WRONG was the causation: they thought germs were a SYMPTOM of disease, not its cause. So: germs visible, link to disease misread.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Microorganism' = a tiny living thing visible only under a microscope (a germ is one kind). 'A product of the illness' = something the illness CREATES, an effect of being sick. 'The source of disease' = what CAUSES the disease. The passage flips these: germs were thought to be the product, not the source.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'Not much was known because it was impossible to observe organisms as small as germs' — contradicts the text; Leeuwenhoek and others DID observe them. B 'Germs had been observed but were not thought to be at all related to disease' — too strong; the text says they WERE linked, just in the wrong direction (product not cause). C 'The germs found in blood were not linked to the organisms that had been found in water' — the passage never claims this separation. D 'The organisms found in blood were not seen as the source of disease' — matches step 3's restatement exactly: seen, but seen wrongly as product not source.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is D. The pre-1800 story isn't 'germs were invisible' — it's 'germs were visible but misread as effect rather than cause'. D names that misreading precisely.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'what was known/thought before X' items, look for a sentence with a CONCESSIVE structure ('Although... they believed...'). That hinge sentence almost always carries the answer — it names what was seen versus how it was interpreted.

pregrade_tactic.move: On conclusion items, when you find a 'some say... others say...' sentence, the answer is usually that the field is divided — not that one side wins.

distractors.why_tempting: If you skim 'parents... freak-out' and 'separate smartphones from older screen-based media', you might read the paragraph as a complaint that parents aren't paying enough attention.
distractors.why_wrong: The text describes parents as concerned across every generation of technology — far from turning a blind eye, they have been 'handwringing' about each new screen. Step 3's restatement has no claim about parental neglect.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that 'smartphones combined with the allure of social media' assigns blame to the device, so 'mobile devices are unfairly blamed' feels like a natural reaction.
distractors.why_wrong: The text never says smartphones are blamed UNFAIRLY — it just notes that the combination of devices and social media is hard for parents and kids. Step 5's option-by-option pass shows C inserts a judgement ('unfairly') that the paragraph never makes.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'too soon to ring alarm bells' as 'the risks are exaggerated' — same family of ideas.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Too soon to ring alarm bells' is the view of ONE camp; the OTHER camp in the same sentence says we DO know enough to restrict phone habits. The paragraph reports both views; it doesn't endorse 'wildly exaggerated'. Step 2's locate step shows the split.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: When a passage presents two opposing views, it is tempting to pick the option that matches the side you sympathize with. The correct option will usually NAME the disagreement itself, not endorse one camp.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The 'Kids and Smartphones' paragraph reports a split: 'some experts say it's too soon to ring alarm bells about smartphones, others argue that we understand enough... to recommend restricting kids' escalating phone habits.' Two camps, no agreement — that is exactly B's 'no consensus yet'. Answer B.

steps.text: 'What can be concluded here?' asks for the option whose claim is best supported by the whole paragraph — not the most provocative, the most CONSISTENT one. Read for the structural takeaway, not for a single vivid phrase.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The paragraph ends on a deliberate split: 'while some experts say it's too soon to ring alarm bells about smartphones, others argue that we understand enough about young people's vulnerabilities to recommend restricting kids' escalating phone habits.' That 'while... others...' construction is the spine of the conclusion.
steps.title: Locate the closing sentences

steps.text: Researchers disagree. One camp says: hold on, we don't have enough data yet to panic. The other camp says: we already know enough to start restricting kids' phone use. So the field has not landed on a single answer about smartphone risk.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Consensus' = general agreement among a group. 'Ring alarm bells' = start warning loudly about danger. 'Vulnerabilities' = weak points, ways someone can be harmed. 'No consensus' means experts have not converged on a single view — exactly what the 'while... others...' sentence describes.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'Parents have too often turned a blind eye to children's media habits' — the text actually describes parents 'freaking out' across generations, the opposite of looking the other way. B 'There is as yet no consensus among researchers on the risks of smartphone use by teenagers' — directly matches the 'some... others...' split in step 2. C 'Mobile devices are being unfairly blamed for young people's obsession with social media' — the text never argues smartphones are blamed UNFAIRLY; it just notes the combination with social media. D 'The risks of children's smartphone exposure have been wildly exaggerated' — only ONE of the two camps would say this; the paragraph itself doesn't take that side.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. The paragraph's whole arc is researchers split into two camps — and that lack of agreement is exactly what 'no consensus' names. The other options pick one side of the split or import judgements the text never makes.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'what can be concluded' items, look for a passage that ends on a 'while X... others Y...' or 'some say... others say...' construction. That balanced split usually points to a 'no consensus / debate continues' option — the writer is reporting the disagreement, not picking a side.

pregrade_tactic.move: On 'implied' items, restate the relevant sentence in the simplest form possible, and pick the option that says the same thing in other words.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read fast, the line 'Darwin... was intrigued by the parallels between linguistic and physical evolution' could suggest the Paris Society quickly embraced the idea.
distractors.why_wrong: The very next sentence says 'The Society, with Catholic leanings, would have none of it.' Step 2's locate step shows the Society BANNED the topic — the opposite of 'immediately realised its evolutionary beginnings'.

distractors.why_tempting: Darwin is a famous name. It's tempting to pair 'Origin of Species' with 'solving the problem of language' since the passage mentions both.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says Darwin was INTERESTED in language evolution, not that he solved it. In fact step 3's restatement is that the topic stalled for 100+ years — the opposite of 'solved once and for all'.

distractors.why_tempting: The Catholic leanings of the Paris Society make the religious angle feel relevant — 'religious circles' sounds like a clean continuation of that thread.
distractors.why_wrong: The Catholic detail is a 19th-century explanation for the 1866 ban, not a description of TODAY's debate. Step 5's option-by-option pass shows D shifts the time-frame from 1866 to 'today' without textual support.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: 'Early on in the text' is a scope cue — it tells you to anchor in the first paragraphs only. If your matched evidence comes from later sections of the passage (e.g. Pinker, Bloom, Chomsky), you are out of scope.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: Early in 'On the Origin of Language', the Paris Linguistic Society banned discussion of language origins in 1866, and 'For more than a century afterwards, little was learned about the evolution of language' — i.e. findings on its roots stayed rare through the 1900s. That is B.

steps.text: 'What is implied EARLY ON in the text about the emergence of human language?' — so focus on the first paragraphs only. The question is testing whether you can read the historical setup: how language origins were treated from 1866 onward.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two sentences set up the early history. First: in 1866 the Paris Linguistic Society banned communications about language origins. Second, the consequence sentence: 'For more than a century afterwards, little was learned about the evolution of language — even though evolution had become the standard explanation for nearly all biological phenomena.'
steps.title: Locate the early-text sentences

steps.text: The Paris ban shut down the topic in 1866, and for the next 100+ years — i.e. through most of the 20th century — almost nothing was figured out about how language evolved. That stayed true even though evolution was being used everywhere else in biology. So: scarce findings for a long time after 1866.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Imply' = state indirectly, leave for the reader to infer. 'For more than a century afterwards' = for over 100 years from 1866 — that runs straight through the 20th century. 'Little was learned' = findings were rare. So 'rare during the 20th century' is the direct paraphrase.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'The Linguistic Society of Paris immediately realised its evolutionary beginnings' — the opposite of what the passage says; the Society BANNED the topic. B 'Findings concerning its roots were rare during the 20th century' — 'For more than a century afterwards, little was learned' converts cleanly to 'rare during the 20th century'. C 'Darwin's theory was credited with having solved the problem once and for all' — the passage says Darwin was 'intrigued' but the Society shut the topic down; nobody credits Darwin with solving it. D 'Its early evolutionary history is today a concern mainly in religious circles' — the text mentions 'Catholic leanings' to explain the 1866 ban, not to describe today's debate.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. 'Little was learned... for more than a century after 1866' is exactly 'findings were rare during the 20th century'. The other options either contradict the passage or smuggle in details from later sections.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'implied' questions, paraphrase the relevant sentence into the simplest possible claim ('almost nothing was learned for 100+ years from 1866') and then look for the option that re-says it in different vocabulary. The correct answer is usually a translation, not a new idea.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the text says 'no consensus' or 'hardly any agreement', pick the option that describes the field as split — not the one that names a winning view.

distractors.why_tempting: Modern science often is driven by brain imaging, and the passage talks about 'mental phenomena' — A feels like a contemporary-sounding fit.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage stresses the OPPOSITE: 'Speech leaves no fossils', and the difficulty of the question is precisely the LACK of hard evidence. Step 5's option-by-option pass shows A invents an evidence base the text never claims.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'There is hardly even the barest agreement that it evolved at all' and read it as 'linguists reject Darwin'.
distractors.why_wrong: The sentence describes a SPLIT, not a majority rejection — Pinker and Bloom are pro-Darwinian, Chomsky's later view is different, others argue about gesture. B turns a debate into a majority rejection. Step 2's locate step keeps the split intact.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'still no consensus' as 'few people care' — if a topic is unresolved, maybe nobody thinks it matters.
distractors.why_wrong: Directly contradicts 'Today, the debate is lively.' Lively is the opposite of 'only a few'. Step 3's restatement preserves the activity of the field.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: Be careful with options that name a specific evidence base ('brain research', 'fossil record', 'cross-linguistic data'). Unless the passage explicitly cites that evidence as foundational, the option is importing detail.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: On current views the passage says: 'Today, the debate is lively. But there is still no consensus on how, when or why language evolved. There is hardly even the barest agreement that it evolved at all.' Lively debate with no consensus = C, 'no clearly dominant opinion'. Answer C.

steps.text: The prompt asks what is claimed about CURRENT views on language origin. So you want the option that names today's state of the field — active or quiet, agreed or split. Hold the present tense.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Three sentences carry the current state. 'Today, the debate is lively.' 'But there is still no consensus on how, when or why language evolved.' 'There is hardly even the barest agreement that it evolved at all, in the sense of having been the specific product of gradual natural selection.'
steps.title: Locate the 'today' sentences

steps.text: Right now researchers are talking a lot about language origins, but they cannot agree on the basics — not on how, not on when, not on why, not even on whether evolution by natural selection is the right frame. So: active discussion, no settled view.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Consensus' = general agreement. 'Lively debate' = active disagreement, lots of voices. 'No clearly dominant opinion' (option C) is the natural translation: many voices, none of them on top. The pair 'lively + no consensus' converts directly to 'no dominant opinion'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'They are based on new evidence derived from modern brain research' — the passage stresses how little hard evidence there is ('Speech leaves no fossils'); nothing says modern views are based on brain research. B 'Most linguists appear reluctant to accept Darwin's perspective' — too strong; the passage describes a split, not a majority rejection. C 'There seems to be no clearly dominant opinion on the issues involved' — matches 'no consensus' directly. D 'Only a few linguists find it an urgent research question' — the text says the debate is LIVELY, the opposite of 'only a few'.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is C. 'No consensus' + 'lively debate' is exactly 'no clearly dominant opinion'. The other options either contradict 'lively' or invent a basis (brain research, Darwin-rejection) the passage does not claim.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-034.technique: When a passage uses 'no consensus' or 'hardly any agreement', look for an option that paraphrases the field as plural or divided — 'no dominant view', 'still being debated', 'mixed opinions'. Options that name ONE prevailing view will be wrong on questions like this.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the question asks about a named researcher's view, find the paragraph that describes that person — the answer is often a synonym-paraphrase of one sentence there.

distractors.why_tempting: The passage talks a lot about the 19th century (Darwin, Paris Society), so 19th-century linguists feel like nearby furniture.
distractors.why_wrong: The 19th-century linguists in this passage are the ones BANNED from discussing language origins; they didn't hold a Darwinian view about language. Step 2's locate step puts Pinker-Bloom firmly in the 1990 Darwinian camp, not the 1866 Catholic-ban camp.

distractors.why_tempting: If you misread 'surprisingly controversial' as 'against Darwin', B looks like the contrarian option that fits.
distractors.why_wrong: Their article was controversial because it ARGUED FOR Darwinian evolution at a place (Chomsky's MIT) where the topic had been off-limits. They added arguments FOR Darwin, not against. Step 3's restatement keeps the direction straight.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that two MIT linguists writing in 1990 would be influenced by MIT's most famous linguist, Noam Chomsky.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says Chomsky 'initially kept mum on the subject' — he wasn't expressing scepticism, he was abstaining. Pinker-Bloom's piece was controversial because it broke the silence, not because it followed Chomsky. Step 5's option-by-option pass catches this.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: MIT + Chomsky in the same paragraph makes it tempting to assume Pinker and Bloom were Chomsky's followers. The passage says the opposite: their article was 'surprisingly controversial' precisely because it broke with Chomsky's silence on the topic.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: Pinker and Bloom's 1990 article argued that language 'had of course evolved in a typically Darwinian fashion: slowly, first as a result of random genetic mutation, but gradually giving early humans a survival advantage' — and they were 'confident in positing a gradual unfolding'. Stepwise = D.

steps.text: The prompt asks specifically about Pinker and Bloom — so find the paragraph that names them, then identify their VIEW on how language evolved. Stay inside the Pinker-Bloom paragraph; don't borrow from Chomsky's later view further down.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: 'In 1990, Steven Pinker and Paul Bloom, both then at MIT, Chomsky's home turf, published an article making a surprisingly controversial case. In their view, language had of course evolved in a typically Darwinian fashion: slowly, first as a result of random genetic mutation, but gradually giving early humans a survival advantage.' And the closer: 'Pinker and Bloom were confident in positing a gradual unfolding.'
steps.title: Locate the Pinker-Bloom paragraph

steps.text: Pinker and Bloom argued: language evolved the normal Darwin way — slowly, in small genetic changes, each step giving humans a bit more survival advantage. They said this confidently. 'Slowly + gradually + step by step' is the core of their view.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Stepwise development' (option D) = development in small steps, one stage at a time — a direct synonym for 'gradual unfolding' and 'evolved slowly'. 'Typically Darwinian fashion' = classic evolution by natural selection, accumulating small changes. So D's wording lines up.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'similar to that of most 19th-century linguists' — the text describes 19th-century linguists as banned from the topic by the Paris Society, not as Darwinian; the comparison is unsupported. B 'fresh arguments AGAINST Darwin's idea of an evolutionary origin' — directly contradicts the passage, which says they argued IN FAVOUR of a Darwinian, evolutionary origin. C 'much influenced by the early scepticism expressed by Noam Chomsky' — Chomsky was at MIT and 'kept mum' on the topic, but the passage describes Pinker-Bloom's view as 'surprisingly controversial' precisely because it broke from Chomsky's silence, not because it followed it. D 'supported the theory of a stepwise development of language' — matches 'gradual unfolding' / 'evolved slowly' directly.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is D. 'Stepwise development' is the natural translation of 'gradual unfolding' and 'slowly, by random genetic mutation, giving survival advantage'. The other options either invert their view (B) or invent influences the text does not claim (A, C).
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-035.technique: When a question targets named researchers, the passage usually contains a paragraph dedicated to them. Find it, then find the simplest one-sentence description of their view. The correct option will be a synonym swap on that one sentence — not a synthesis from elsewhere in the passage.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the text uses words like 'single', 'sudden' or 'all at once', pick the option that echoes that exact nuance — the words in the text are usually direct clues.

distractors.why_tempting: Pinker and Bloom (in the previous paragraph) talk about survival advantage from communication. It's easy to slot Chomsky into the same 'language is for communicating' frame.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage is explicit and almost shouts the opposite: Merge 'was not for communication, but for thought'. Step 2's locate step pulls that exact line; A inverts Chomsky's main claim.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Combining ideas' is the heart of Merge, and 'paved the way for' is a soft, hedging verb that sounds compatible with almost any cause-effect description.
distractors.why_wrong: Merge IS the ability to combine ideas; it did not PRECEDE that ability or enable some later development of it. Step 3's restatement makes Merge the direct cause of combinatorial thought, not a runway for it.

distractors.why_tempting: The passage spends a paragraph on birds and chimps, so 'similar to certain types of animal language' feels like a continuation of that thread.
distractors.why_wrong: The animal-cousins paragraph is a separate section about a different research direction; it never describes Chomsky's Merge as similar to animal calls. Step 5's option-by-option pass shows D moves Chomsky into a paragraph that isn't his.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: Option B ('paved the way for the human potential to combine ideas') sounds nearly identical to Chomsky's view but is subtly wrong on timing. Merge IS the combining ability, not its precursor. When an option uses 'paved the way' or 'enabled', check whether the trait is the cause or the thing itself.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: Chomsky's later view, per the passage: 'a single mutation in a single human gave that human an ability called Merge.' One mutation, one human, expanded mental capacity — that is C's 'sudden genetic change expanding human mental capacity'.

steps.text: The prompt asks about Chomsky's MORE RECENT view of language. So skip his earlier 'kept mum' stage and find the paragraph where the passage describes the new position he eventually arrived at.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: 'Noam Chomsky later came to an unusual and different solution: that a single mutation in a single human gave that human an ability called Merge. This ability was not for communication, but for thought: it allowed simpler thoughts to be combined into larger and more complex ones, and this complex thought was the real survival advantage.'
steps.title: Locate Chomsky's recent-view paragraph

steps.text: Chomsky now thinks language did not creep in step by step. Instead a single genetic mutation in one person produced a new mental ability ('Merge') that let humans combine small thoughts into bigger, more complex ones. The big payoff was for THINKING, with language as a useful side effect. So: one sudden mutation, bigger mental capacity.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Mutation' = a genetic change. 'A single mutation in a single human' = one sudden change, in one person — the opposite of gradual evolution. 'Mental capacity' = the brain's ability to do things (think, combine ideas). 'Sudden genetic change' (option C) is the direct synonym for 'a single mutation'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'arose in response to communicative pressure in early communities' — the passage explicitly says Merge 'was NOT for communication, but for thought'. B 'paved the way for the human potential to combine ideas' — close, but reverses the logic: Merge IS the ability to combine ideas, it does not PAVE THE WAY for that ability; the combining ability was the direct gift, not an aftermath. C 'resulted from a sudden genetic change expanding human mental capacity' — 'single mutation' = 'sudden genetic change'; 'allowed simpler thoughts to be combined into larger and more complex ones' = 'expanding mental capacity'. Match. D 'Its initial manifestations were similar to certain types of animal language' — the animal-cousins discussion is a separate paragraph; Chomsky's specific view never compares Merge to animal calls.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is C. 'Single mutation in a single human' + 'expanded the ability to combine thoughts' is exactly 'a sudden genetic change expanding human mental capacity'. The other options either reverse cause and effect (B), invert his claim (A), or borrow from the animal-cousins paragraph (D).
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-036.technique: When a named scientist's view is described with words like 'single', 'sudden', 'one', 'all at once' — look for an option that contains 'sudden' or 'abrupt'. The wording is the giveaway: gradualists never get described as 'single mutation' people.

pregrade_tactic.move: On writer's-impression items, focus on the last paragraph and hunt for 'or it can be taken as' — what follows 'or' is often the writer's own reading.

distractors.why_tempting: Modern research, MIT scientists, 'lively debate' — the surface of the passage feels like progress.
distractors.why_wrong: Earlier in the passage the writer notes 'little was learned' for over a century and 'still no consensus' today. Step 2's locate step shows the writer treats the unresolved state as the headline, not the progress.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'could be seen as a scientific failure' and read that as the writer's view.
distractors.why_wrong: That phrase is half of a 'could be seen as X — OR taken as Y' construction. The writer explicitly prefers the second half ('charming reminder'). Step 3's restatement shows the writer is not endorsing the 'failure' reading.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'wildly different accounts' as 'some of these scientists are wrong'.
distractors.why_wrong: The writer doesn't blame the linguists; the writer blames the question. The disagreement is framed as a property of the SUBJECT, not a failing of the researchers. Step 5's option-by-option pass catches the misdirected blame.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: Option C ('unexpected as well as utterly disappointing') matches the FIRST half of the writer's closing — 'could be seen as a scientific failure'. The writer offers that reading only to reject it. When a passage uses 'could be seen as X — OR Y', the Y is what the writer endorses.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The closing paragraph reframes the disagreement: language origins remain hard 'just because they are humankind's most human traits. Nobody ever said that studying the fascinating but flawed human mind with that very same mind should be easy.' The writer's takeaway is that complex mental phenomena are inherently hard to study — A.

steps.text: The prompt asks for the WRITER'S BASIC IMPRESSION — the overall stance the article takes on language-origins research. So focus on the closing paragraph, where writers usually deposit their summary judgement.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: 'That so many great minds, including household names like Pinker and Chomsky, give such wildly different accounts, could be seen as a scientific failure. Or it can be taken as a charming reminder that even if science has left creation myths and just-so stories behind in the past, some problems – like consciousness, as well as language – remain hard to solve just because they are humankind's most human traits. Nobody ever said that studying the fascinating but flawed human mind with that very same mind should be easy.'
steps.title: Locate the closing sentences

steps.text: The writer offers two readings of the disagreement: you COULD call it a failure, OR you can call it a charming reminder that some questions — especially about the human mind — are genuinely hard because we are studying ourselves with ourselves. The 'or' second reading is the one the writer endorses with the final sentence. So: language origins are hard because they touch the deepest, most mental, most human stuff.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Difficulties involved in studying complex mental phenomena' (option A) = exactly 'studying the fascinating but flawed human mind with that very same mind' — the same idea in plainer words. 'Mental phenomena' = mind-things like thought, consciousness, language. 'Complex' = with many interacting parts.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'a good example of the difficulties involved in studying complex mental phenomena' — paraphrases the closing 'hard because they are humankind's most human traits' / 'studying the flawed human mind with that same mind'. Match. B 'significant progress has been made since the mid-19th century' — the passage actually says 'For more than a century afterwards, little was learned' and the field is now 'lively' but without consensus; progress is mixed at best, not 'significant'. C 'unexpected as well as utterly disappointing' — too harsh; the writer explicitly REJECTS calling the disagreement a failure ('Or it can be taken as a charming reminder...'). D 'even prominent linguists can go wrong when faced with the complexity of some languages' — the writer does not say Pinker / Chomsky 'go wrong'; the writer says they offer different accounts because the topic itself is hard.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is A. The writer reads the disagreement among great minds as evidence that complex mental phenomena are hard to study from the inside — a difficulty story, not a failure story. A names that exact framing.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'writer's impression' items, prioritize the closing paragraph and look for an 'Or it can be taken as...' style hedge. The clause AFTER the 'or' is usually the writer's preferred reading; pick the option that matches that clause, not the one before it.

pregrade_tactic.move: On 'implied' items featuring words like 'partial', 'in general' or 'seems to', pick the option that names the gap in knowledge — the hedge is the answer.

distractors.why_tempting: If you scan the list of biological functions and stop at 'immune system', it's easy to assume losing sleep wrecks immunity completely.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly denies this: 'none of these functions fails completely in the absence of sleep.' Step 2's locate step pulls that exact line; A flips it into an absolute that the text rejects.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'why do people die without sleep?' is to grab the most dramatic mechanism mentioned — toxin clearance from the brain.
distractors.why_wrong: Toxin clearance is listed as ONE among many sleep-enhanced functions, never as the MAIN cause of fatal sleep deprivation. The passage stops short of naming the mechanism — that is precisely the open question. Step 3's restatement keeps the why-gap intact.

distractors.why_tempting: Sleep deprivation is a familiar modern complaint; D feels like a natural lifestyle takeaway.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph is about why sleep is biologically necessary, not about how much people actually sleep. Step 5's option-by-option pass shows D shifts the topic from biology to behaviour.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: C sounds scientific (toxin accumulation in the brain) and the passage does mention toxin clearance as one function of sleep. But naming a SPECIFIC fatal mechanism overreaches: the passage explicitly says the deeper 'why' is unresolved.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The 'Sleeping' paragraph says research has only begun 'to provide at least a PARTIAL explanation', that sleep enhances many systems 'instead of being absolutely necessary', and yet 'anyone who lives for months without sleep will die.' Why sleep is necessary is still unsettled — that is B.

steps.text: 'What is implied here?' on a short paragraph means: which option best captures the OVERALL state of knowledge the writer is describing? Read the whole paragraph as one unit and ask what it adds up to.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Opening: 'Research over the past 20 years has finally begun to provide at least a partial explanation for why we must sleep.' Middle: 'none of these functions fails completely in the absence of sleep. In general, sleep seems to enhance the performance of these systems instead of being absolutely necessary.' Closing: 'And yet anyone who lives for months without sleep will die.'
steps.title: Locate the framing sentences

steps.text: We know sleep helps many systems (immunity, hormones, mood, memory, brain cleanup), but none of those systems totally fails when you don't sleep. So 'helps' isn't enough to explain why we MUST sleep. Yet without sleep, people die. The paragraph ends on a puzzle: we have a partial explanation, the deep 'why' is unresolved.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Satisfactory account' (option B) = an explanation that fully answers the question. 'Partial explanation' (in the passage) is exactly the opposite of a 'satisfactory account'. 'Why sleep is necessary for humans to survive' is the gap the paragraph names but does not close.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'Staying awake for a long time will lead to a collapse of your immune system' — the passage specifically denies this: 'none of these functions fails completely in the absence of sleep.' B 'There is still no satisfactory account of why sleep is necessary for humans to survive' — matches 'partial explanation' + 'and yet anyone... will die' exactly: the deep why is open. C 'Lack of sleep is fatal mainly due to the accumulation of poisonous substances in your brain' — the passage lists toxin clearance as ONE of many functions sleep enhances, but never says toxin build-up is the MAIN cause of fatal sleep deprivation. D 'most people do not sleep as much as they should in order to function optimally' — the paragraph is about WHY sleep matters, not about how much people sleep.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. 'Partial explanation' + 'must sleep to survive' = an open question. B names the open question; the other options either contradict the passage (A, C) or move the topic off-axis (D).
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On 'implied' items with hedged language ('partial', 'in general', 'seems to', 'at least'), prefer an option that names the GAP in knowledge rather than the substance of what's known. The writer's hedges are the answer signal.

pregrade_tactic.move: Trace cause and effect through the paragraph — the right option names the link everything passes through, not a side-link.

distractors.why_tempting: Flooding + food scarcity + Nile is a familiar disaster story, and the passage does pair those terms.
distractors.why_wrong: The disaster here is REDUCED flooding (volcanic dimming cut the summer flood), not excessive flooding. Step 2's locate step shows 'reduced the summer flooding needed for crop growth' — A flips the sign.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Ancient texts' and 'modern climate science' as sources sound dramatic, so 'spectacular tales of volcanic activity in old manuscripts' looks like a fair description of the evidence.
distractors.why_wrong: The texts are RESEARCH MATERIAL, not the topic the paragraph emphasizes. Step 3's restatement shows the load-bearing circumstance is the Nile / crops / society chain, not the manuscripts.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'climate change' in the passage and reach for the modern association — humans causing the change.
distractors.why_wrong: The climate change here is caused by VOLCANIC eruptions, a natural cause. The passage explicitly names volcanoes as the trigger. Step 5's option-by-option pass shows C imports a human cause the text never claims.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: Watch the DIRECTION of the cause: 'reduced flooding' is the opposite of 'excessive flooding'. Quick scanners often pair 'Nile' + 'flood' + 'disaster' and pick whichever option mentions all three, missing the sign of the change.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The 'Ancient Egypt' paragraph anchors the whole story in the Nile: volcanic eruptions reduced 'the summer flooding needed for crop growth', which caused famine and riots — i.e. the Nile's flooding was what fed Egyptian agriculture and stability. That is D, 'the importance of the Nile for agriculture and society at large.'

steps.text: 'What SPECIFIC CIRCUMSTANCE is emphasized in this text?' — so look for the single concrete fact the paragraph keeps pointing at. Not a vague theme, a specific circumstance.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The paragraph's payoff: volcanic eruptions 'reduced the summer flooding needed for crop growth', which caused 'famine and riots'. So the chain is: volcano → less Nile flooding → no crops → hunger → unrest → collapse.
steps.title: Locate the central sentence

steps.text: The whole story hangs on the Nile. Egyptian agriculture depended on the Nile flooding every summer; when volcanoes disrupted that flooding, crops failed, people starved, riots followed, and the society fell apart. The Nile is the load-bearing detail in every step.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Summer flooding needed for crop growth' = the Nile's annual flood was the basis of Egyptian farming. 'Society at large' (option D) = the population in general, including everyday life and food supply. The text moves from 'crops' (agriculture) to 'famine and riots' (society) — exactly the agriculture + society pairing in D.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'link between scarcity of food and EXCESSIVE flooding of the Nile' — wrong direction; the disaster was REDUCED flooding, not too much. B 'spectacular tales of volcanic activity found in old manuscripts' — the passage uses 'ancient texts' as a research source, not as 'spectacular tales'; this misframes the methodology as the subject. C 'disastrous effects of human-caused climate change FROM LONG AGO' — the climate change here is caused by VOLCANOES, not by humans; C imports a modern frame. D 'the importance of the Nile for agriculture and society at large' — captures the chain: Nile flood → crops → society. Match.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is D. Every link in the paragraph's causal chain runs through the Nile, and the consequences sweep from crops to riots — exactly 'agriculture and society at large'. The other options flip the direction of the flooding (A), misread the texts as the subject (B), or attribute the climate change to humans (C).
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On 'what specific circumstance is emphasized' items, trace the CAUSAL CHAIN in the paragraph. The correct option will name the link that everything else passes through. Here, every cause and every effect runs through the Nile — that is the load-bearing circumstance.

pregrade_tactic.move: On experiment paragraphs, find the sentence that reports the result — usually right after the method — and pick the option that says the same thing plainly.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'inferior frontal cortex' and 'superior temporal gyrus' but blur the timing, you might assume both regions are doing the same job at the same time.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage names two SEPARATE regions doing two DIFFERENT jobs with a 0.2-second offset — prediction comes first in one region, then sound-processing in the other. Step 2's locate step keeps them apart.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that the brain must process the raw sound before it can understand the word — that matches everyday intuition.
distractors.why_wrong: The experiment shows the opposite: the prediction region fires 0.2 seconds BEFORE the sound region. Word-level guessing precedes sound-level processing here, not the other way around. Step 3's restatement preserves this ordering.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the word 'process sounds' from the experiment and miss the word 'predicts', D's flat 'never uses meaning to infer sounds' might pass as a summary.
distractors.why_wrong: The whole point of the study is that the brain DOES use word-level prediction (a meaning-driven process) to infer missing sounds. D inverts the finding. Step 5's option-by-option pass catches the inversion.

host-2022-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: When a passage describes TWO brain regions with different jobs and different timing, options that collapse them into one ('same region') or that flip the order ('all sounds before words') are nearly always wrong. The separation is usually the whole point.
host-2022-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The 'Word Processing' paragraph reports that when researchers replaced part of a word with noise, the volunteers' brains 'responded as if they had actually heard the missing s or c sound' — the brain effectively produced a sound that wasn't there. That is A.

steps.text: 'What are we told here?' is a 'main claim' question: pick the option whose statement is directly reported by the text — not what you can almost-infer, but what the paragraph actually says.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The key sentences: 'The volunteers were played recordings of a word that could either be faster or factor, with the middle sound replaced by noise. Data from the electrodes showed that their brains responded as if they had actually heard the missing s or c sound.' And the mechanism: 'the inferior frontal cortex region of the brain predicts what word someone is likely to hear, two-tenths of a second before the superior temporal gyrus starts processing the sounds a person has heard.'
steps.title: Locate the experiment's finding

steps.text: When the middle of a word was replaced with noise, the brain still 'heard' the missing letter — it filled in a sound that had never reached the ears. A prediction region in the brain guesses the word a fraction of a second BEFORE the sound region processes what actually came in. So: the brain can manufacture sounds that were never physically there.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Process sounds that were never there in the first place' (option A) = produce or register sounds that did not enter the ear — exactly the 'fill in inaudible sections of speech' framing from the opening line and the 'responded as if they had actually heard the missing s or c' finding. 'Fill in' = supply something that is missing.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'The brain may process sounds that were never there in the first place' — matches 'fill in inaudible sections' + 'brains responded as if they had actually heard the missing s or c'. B 'The brain seems to process sounds and meanings in the same region' — the passage names TWO different regions (inferior frontal cortex for prediction, superior temporal gyrus for sound processing); same-region claim contradicts that. C 'The brain processes all sounds before it begins processing words' — the passage says the opposite: the WORD-prediction region fires 0.2 seconds BEFORE the sound region; word-processing leads sound-processing here. D 'The brain never uses meaning to infer the individual sounds of words' — directly contradicts the entire finding; the brain IS using prediction (a meaning-driven process) to infer sounds.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is A. The experiment's headline finding is exactly 'the brain fills in sounds that were never physically heard'. The other options invert the timing (C, D) or collapse the two brain regions the passage carefully separates (B).
steps.title: Conclusion

host-2022-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On short experiment paragraphs, find the sentence that reports the RESULT (usually after the methodology). Restate the result in one plain sentence and pick the option that matches it. The other options often invert direction or merge things the text keeps separate.

pregrade_tactic.move: När frågan handlar om vad X säger eller menar, leta efter meningen där X införs och stanna där — allt annat i texten kan vara distraktion.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är frestande att tänka att masskultur betyder "för massorna" och därför är folkligt förankrad.
distractors.why_wrong: Ordet "mass" syftar här på publiken (massan som konsumerar), inte på produktionsledet. Som steg 3 visar är hela Groys poäng att produktionen var koncentrerad till ett fåtal medan konsumtionen var massiv — folklig förankring i produktionen är just vad masskulturen INTE hade.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid att texten handlar om kritik och reformation och tror att masskulturen attackerar något.
distractors.why_wrong: Det är reformationen som angrep den katolska konsten — inte 1900-talets masskultur. Groys beskriver masskulturen neutralt som en struktur (få producenter, många konsumenter), inte som något angripande.

distractors.why_tempting: Första instinkten kan vara att blanda ihop textens två trådar: Luther var reformatorisk, alltså blir hans efterföljare också reformatoriska och den masskultur de verkar i också det.
distractors.why_wrong: Reformatorisk är Luther och Ramocki — inte 1900-talets masskultur som Groys beskriver. Steg 2 isolerar exakt vad Groys säger om masskulturen: koncentrerad produktion. Inget om reformation.

host-2022-verb1-LÄS-011.pitfall: Det är lätt att blanda Groys beskrivning av det gamla (masskulturen) med hans beskrivning av det nya (internet-eran). Botemedlet: läs meningen där "tidigare" och "idag" möts och pricka av VEM som producerade VAD i varje tidsperiod.
host-2022-verb1-LÄS-011.solution_path: Boris Groys säger att förhållandet mellan producenter och konsumenter har inverterats — tidigare producerade en liten grupp materialet medan resten konsumerade. Konsten utövades alltså av ett fåtal. Svaret är D.

steps.text: Frågan är specifikt om hur Boris Groys karaktäriserar 1900-talets masskultur — alltså HUR det såg ut då, enligt honom. Inte vad samtidskonsten är idag och inte Luthers förhållande till konst, utan Groys beskrivning av det gamla läget.
steps.title: Vad frågar texten?

steps.text: Groys citeras i andra stycket: "det traditionella förhållandet mellan producenter och konsumenter, såsom det yttrade sig i 1900-talets masskultur, har inverterats". Förklaringen kommer direkt efter: "då det tidigare var en liten grupp som producerade allt material som vi konsumerade".
steps.title: Hitta stället

steps.text: På vardagssvenska: förr i tiden — under 1900-talets masskultur — fanns det få producenter och många konsumenter. En liten klick gjorde böckerna, filmerna, bilderna; alla andra konsumerade det de gjorde. Det är den gamla bilden som Groys säger har vänts upp och ner.
steps.title: Parafrasera

steps.text: A "folklig förankring" — texten beskriver tvärtom en pyramidstruktur där folket bara konsumerade, inte producerade. B "angrep kulturen" — masskulturen i Groys mening VAR kulturen, den angrep den inte. C "reformatorisk" — det är Luther, inte 1900-talets masskultur. D "utövades av ett fåtal" — exakt vad "en liten grupp som producerade allt" betyder.
steps.title: Matcha mot alternativen

steps.text: Svaret är D. Groys poäng är att produktionen förr satt hos ett fåtal — den är precis det som internet enligt honom har spräckt, så att vem som helst nu kan producera konst.
steps.title: Slutsats

host-2022-verb1-LÄS-011.technique: I citerande LÄS-frågor — där texten återger en annans tanke — leta efter den exakta meningen där tanken införs och stanna där. Allt annat i texten kan vara distraktion. Triggern: "enligt X" eller "Y menar att" → spåra vad just X/Y säger, inte vad textens egen författare tycker.

pregrade_tactic.move: Står två namn i frågan, leta efter meningen där författaren själv ställer dem bredvid varandra — ord som "liksom" eller "i efterföljd" markerar parallellen.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är lätt att läsa Luthers angrepp på den katolska kyrkan som ett angrepp på "stelnade strukturer" och anta att Ramockis kommentar till konstvärlden är detsamma.
distractors.why_wrong: Texten säger inget om att Ramocki kritiserar några stelnade strukturer — han beskrivs som någon som har "en lättsam konversation om konstnärens roll". Som steg 5 visar är A en gissning som lägger på Luthers profil på Ramocki utan textstöd.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid Groys citat om hur masskulturen har inverterats och drar slutsatsen att både Luther och Ramocki driver den förändringen.
distractors.why_wrong: Groys är den som beskriver inversionen — inte Ramocki eller Luther själva. Steg 2 visar att textens parallell mellan Luther och Ramocki ligger i teknikvalet, inte i någon delad agenda om masskultur.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar är ofta att texten handlar om konst, alltså måste det de delar vara något konstnärligt.
distractors.why_wrong: Luther är reformator, inte konstnär — han betraktade till och med konsten fientligt i början. Det går inte att jämföra deras syn på konstnärens uppgift när den ene inte är konstnär. Steg 5 hänvisar parallellen tillbaka till teknik, inte konstnärsroll.

host-2022-verb1-LÄS-012.pitfall: Det är lätt att blanda ihop vad texten SÄGER om gemensamheten med vad man själv kan TÄNKA SIG vara gemen- samt. Botemedlet: stryk under den enda meningen där författaren kopplar de två personerna — och godta inget alternativ som inte kan stödjas av den meningen.
host-2022-verb1-LÄS-012.solution_path: Texten parallelliserar Luthers boktryckarkonst med Ramockis Twitter — båda använde DAGENS nyaste medieteknik för att sprida sina teser. Gemensamt: nyttjandet av ny teknik. Svaret är C.

steps.text: Vi ska hitta vad de två personerna har GEMENSAMT enligt texten — alltså en uttalad parallell mellan Luther och Ramocki som textförfattaren själv drar. Inte gissningar, utan något texten faktiskt påstår att de delar.
steps.title: Vad frågar texten?

steps.text: Parallellen dras explicit i andra styckets början: "Boktryckarkonsten var en relativt ny teknik ... Om Luther hade levt idag skulle han förmodligen ha använt sig av dagens teknik". Direkt efter introduceras Ramocki som någon som "använt Twitter för att 'spika upp' sina teser i Luthers efterföljd".
steps.title: Hitta stället

steps.text: På vardagssvenska: Luther använde sin tids nya teknik (boktryckarkonsten) för att sprida budskap. Ramocki använder vår tids nya teknik (Twitter) för att sprida sina teser. Det är samma rörelse i två olika århundraden — välj den nyaste tekniken som finns.
steps.title: Parafrasera

steps.text: Texten säger uttryckligen att Ramocki spikar upp sina teser "i Luthers efterföljd". Det är textens eget ord för vad de har gemen- samt — Ramocki gör samma sak som Luther gjorde, fast med modernare medel. Det är inte en gissning vi gör, det är textens egen koppling.
steps.title: Vad menas med "i Luthers efterföljd"?

steps.text: A "kritik av stelnade strukturer" — Luther kritiserade kyrkan; om Ramocki kritiserar något står inget om det i texten. B "viljan att förändra masskulturen" — Luther ville inte förändra masskultur (begreppet finns inte än), Ramocki kommenterar den men det sägs inte att han VILL ändra den. C "nyttjandet av ny teknik" — exakt vad parallellen i steg 3 visar. D "synen på konstnärens uppgift" — Luther var ingen konstnär, så ingen jämförelse möjlig.
steps.title: Matcha mot alternativen

steps.text: Svaret är C. Den uttryckliga parallellen i texten ligger på själva tekniknyttjandet — boktryck då, Twitter nu. Allt annat skulle vara mer eller mindre rimliga gissningar, men inget av det stöds direkt av texten.
steps.title: Slutsats

host-2022-verb1-LÄS-012.technique: Parallell-frågor i HP-LÄS — "vad har X och Y gemen- samt?" — löser man genom att hitta den mening där textförfattaren själv ställer dem bredvid varandra. Den parallellen är ofta explicit ("i X:s efterföljd", "liksom X", "på samma sätt"). Triggern: två namn i frågestam- men → leta i texten efter meningen där båda namnen står nära, eller där författaren använder ett efterföljd-ord.

pregrade_tactic.move: Ordet "särskilt" eller "viktigast" pekar dig mot stycket där författaren argumenterar för en specifik funktion — leta efter motiveringen, inte exemplet.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är frestande att fastna vid det målande avsnittet om boksamtal i smågrupper och tro att det är textens huvudpoäng.
distractors.why_wrong: Boksamtalet är ett av flera arbetssätt inom läsfrämjande — ett konkret exempel, inte HUVUDFUNKTIONEN. Steg 2 visar att den centrala argumentlinjen handlar om MIK och undervisning, inte om samtalsledning som sådan.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid orden "kritiskt värdera" och drar slutsatsen att bibliotekarien själv ska vara den värderande instansen.
distractors.why_wrong: Texten säger att eleverna ska lära sig värdera texter — och att bibliotekarien lär ut detta. Funktionen är alltså att UNDERVISA i värdering, inte att vara värderaren. B förväxlar lärarrollen med ämnet.

distractors.why_tempting: Första instinkten är att slutmeningen om att vuxna ska vara "de bästa förebilderna" är textens kärnbudskap, eftersom den står sist.
distractors.why_wrong: Den meningen handlar om ALLA vuxna i skolan ("lärare, bibliotekarier, alla"), inte specifikt om bibliotekariens särskilda funktion. Som steg 5 visar är textens specifika argument för bibliotekarien hennes pedagogiska kompetens, inte förebilds-rollen.

host-2022-verb1-LÄS-013.solution_path: Textförfattaren beskriver bibliotekarier som lärare i medieoch informationskunnighet (MIK) — alltså en pedagogisk resurs som ska undervisa eleverna i något som lärarna ofta inte är utbildade i. Svaret är C.

steps.text: Frågan handlar om vilken funktion textförfattaren SÄRSKILT lyfter fram — alltså huvudfunktionen, inte vilken som helst som omnämns. Vi letar efter den roll som hon argumenterar tydligast för i texten.
steps.title: Vad frågar texten?

steps.text: Den centrala passagen är i tredje stycket: "Lärare är inte per automatik utbildade i [att analysera, kritiskt värdera, söka och hantera information]. Det är däremot bibliotekarier, som därför kan bidra med undervisning i medieoch informationskunnighet (MIK)." Hela poängen är att bibliotekarien fyller en pedagogisk lucka.
steps.title: Hitta stället

steps.text: På vardagssvenska: läroplanen ställer nya krav på att eleverna ska bli bra på att hantera information kritiskt. Lärarna kan ofta inte detta. Bibliotekarierna kan. Alltså ska bibliotekarien faktiskt UNDERVISA eleverna — vara en lärare bredvid läraren, en pedagogisk resurs i klassrummet.
steps.title: Parafrasera

steps.text: En "pedagogisk resurs" är någon som aktivt bidrar till elevernas lärande genom undervisning — inte bara genom att låna ut böcker. Texten upprepar det: "Skolbibliotekarien kan med sin kompetens ge stöd till ett undervisningsområde och arbeta med analysförmåga, begrepp, informationssökning och källkritik." Det är pedagogiskt arbete, inte servicefunktion.
steps.title: Vad menas med "pedagogisk resurs"?

steps.text: A "samtalsledande" — boksamtalet nämns, men det är ett av flera arbetssätt, inte huvudpoängen. B "värderande" — bibliotekarien lär eleverna att kritiskt värdera texter, men själva funktionen är att UNDERVISA i värdering, inte att vara värderare. C "pedagogiska resurser" — exakt vad MIK-undervisning innebär: aktivt deltagande i elevernas lärande. D "läsande förebilder" — texten säger att alla vuxna ska vara läsande förebilder, inte särskilt bibliotekarier.
steps.title: Matcha mot alternativen

steps.text: Svaret är C. Textförfattarens huvudargument är genomgående att skolbibliotekarien ska behandlas som en pedagog — inte bara en bokutlånare — eftersom det är så hennes kompetens fyller en faktisk lucka i undervisningen.
steps.title: Slutsats

host-2022-verb1-LÄS-013.technique: Funktion-frågor i HP-LÄS — "vilken funktion lyfter författaren särskilt fram?" — löser man genom att hitta det stycke där författaren argumenterar för en specifik roll OCH ger motivering för den. Sätt-på-piedestal-stycket är ofta det med ett "därför" eller "den viktigaste förutsättningen är". Triggern: ordet "särskilt" eller "viktigast" → leta efter motiveringen, inte exemplet.

pregrade_tactic.move: När texten visar flera parter som skyller på varandra, är själva ansvarskampen poängen — välj alternativet som beskriver att ingen tar tag i frågan.

distractors.why_tempting: Det är frestande att läsa stycket om bibliotekarier som arbetar på "tre, fyra olika skolor" och tolka det som orsaken till att bibliotek saknas.
distractors.why_wrong: Det att bibliotekarier har för många skolor är en KONSEKVENS av den otydliga plattfor- men — inte en orsak till att skolor saknar bibliotek överhuvudtaget. Steg 2 visar att textens utpekade orsak är ansvarskampen, inte personalfördelningen.

distractors.why_tempting: Många stannar vid den första meningen "trots att det sedan 2011 står i skollagen" och tror att texten skyller på lagstiftarna.
distractors.why_wrong: Skollagen är förutsättningen, inte orsaken till motsatsen. Texten säger inte att lagstiftarna ska genomföra något — den säger att rektorer och huvudmän skyller på varandra. B förlägger ansvaret hos fel aktör.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar är ofta att eftersom rektorerna talar om pengar måste textförfattaren mena att olika ekonomi mellan skolor är orsaken.
distractors.why_wrong: Texten REDOGÖR FÖR vad rektorerna säger — men hon godtar inte påståendet som textens egen förklaring. Som steg 4 visar är hennes egen formulering "en ständig kamp om vem som ska ta ansvar", inte "olika resurser". C blandar ihop ett refererat påstående med textförfattarens uppfattning.
